steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage uses a map-versus-landscape-painting analogy: historical knowledge is like a map — factually accurate but 'cold and naked' — while paintings offer 'mimic charm.' The question asks what claim this analogy best supports.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Historians are more interested in the lives of famous people than in those of the less famous.: the analogy is about vividness vs. facts, not reliability. ✗
  • Choice B — The creativity of historians is restricted by the need to write strictly truthful works.: utility is not the analogy's concern; the contrast is emotional resonance vs. bare fact. ✗
  • Choice C — The lives of historical figures are usually documented as a collection of facts, rather than as a representation of their personalities.: the map gives accurate data but no warmth; the painting gives personality. This is exactly the analogy. ✓
  • Choice D — Paintings may be more beautiful than maps, but maps are more useful than paintings.: misreads the analogy as a literal comparison of maps and paintings rather than a metaphor about historical documentation. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
